question: Explain the fundamental difference between image classification and object detection.
expectedPoints: Image classification assigns a single label to an entire image.
Object detection identifies and localizes multiple objects within an image, providing bounding boxes and class labels for each.
Object detection is a more complex task as it combines classification with localization.

question: Describe the key components and purpose of the Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) algorithm in object detection pipelines.
expectedPoints: Its purpose is to reduce multiple, overlapping bounding boxes for the same object down to a single, most confident detection.
It works by selecting the bounding box with the highest confidence score and suppressing (removing) all other boxes that have a high Intersection-over-Union (IoU) with it, above a set threshold.
This post-processing step is crucial for cleaning up the output of detectors like YOLO or SSD, which often propose many candidate boxes for each object.

question: Compare and contrast the architectural approaches of a classic CNN for image classification (e.g., VGG) and a fully convolutional network (FCN) for semantic segmentation. Explain the purpose of the key components that differ between them.
expectedPoints: A classic CNN uses a series of convolutional and pooling layers to extract features, followed by fully connected (dense) layers at the end to perform the classification. This structure loses spatial information and outputs a single class label.

An FCN removes the final fully connected layers and replaces them with convolutional layers. This allows the network to accept an input of any size and produce a spatial output map (not a single vector).

The FCN uses transposed convolutional layers (or upsampling) to increase the spatial resolution of the feature maps back to the input image size, creating a pixel-wise prediction. Skip connections are often used to combine fine-grained details from earlier layers with the high-level semantic information from deeper layers to produce accurate, detailed segmentation masks.
